sections.heading: Background: Eutyches and Monophysitism
sections.html:
Background: Eutyches and Monophysitism

The Council of Chalcedon (AD 451), the fourth ecumenical council, was the largest and most theologically precise of the early councils, attended by approximately 520 bishops. It produced the Chalcedonian Definition, which remains the Christological standard of Roman Catholicism, Eastern Orthodoxy, and the vast majority of Protestant churches.

The controversy that prompted the council arose after Ephesus. A monk named Eutyches, archimandrite of a large monastery in Constantinople, over-corrected against Nestorianism. To preserve the unity of Christ's person, he denied the ongoing distinction of two natures after the Incarnation. He taught that Christ had two natures before the union but only one after — the divine nature having absorbed or transformed the human. This view, called Monophysitism ("one nature"), implied that Christ's humanity was not fully, genuinely human.

Eutyches was condemned at a local synod in Constantinople (448) but was vindicated at the "Robber Council" of Ephesus (449) — so called by Pope Leo I — where Dioscorus of Alexandria used imperial troops to physically intimidate the bishops. Pope Leo responded with his celebrated Tome (letter to Flavian), a clear statement of the two-natures doctrine, which was read at Chalcedon to acclamation: "Peter has spoken through Leo!"

Emperor Marcian and Empress Pulcheria called the council, which overturned the Robber Council and deposed Dioscorus.

sections.heading: The Chalcedonian Definition
sections.html:
The Chalcedonian Definition

The council's central document is the Definition of Chalcedon — one of the most precisely worded theological statements in Christian history. It charts a middle course between the Nestorian error (two persons) and the Eutychian error (one merged nature):

Following, then, the holy Fathers, we all with one voice teach men to confess one and the same Son, our Lord Jesus Christ, the same perfect in Godhead and also perfect in manhood; truly God and truly man, of a reasonable soul and body; consubstantial with the Father according to the Godhead, and consubstantial with us according to the Manhood; in all things like unto us, without sin; begotten before all ages of the Father according to the Godhead, and in these latter days, for us and for our salvation, born of the Virgin Mary, the Mother of God, according to the Manhood;

one and the same Christ, Son, Lord, Only-begotten, to be acknowledged in two natures, inconfusedly, unchangeably, indivisibly, inseparably; the distinction of natures being by no means taken away by the union, but rather the property of each nature being preserved, and concurring in one Person and one Subsistence, not parted or divided into two persons, but one and the same Son, and only begotten, God the Word, the Lord Jesus Christ — as the prophets from the beginning have declared concerning him, and the Lord Jesus Christ himself taught us, and the Creed of the holy Fathers has handed down to us.

The four famous adverbs "inconfusedly, unchangeably, indivisibly, inseparably" (ἀσυγχύτως, ἀτρέπτως, ἀδιαιρέτως, ἀχωρίστως) define the character of the union by what it is not:

  • Inconfusedly, unchangeably — against Eutychianism: the two natures are not mixed, merged, or transformed into a third thing.
  • Indivisibly, inseparably — against Nestorianism: the two natures cannot be pulled apart into two separate persons.

The result: Christ is one Person (or Subsistence, Greek hypostasis) in two complete, distinct, and unaltered natures — fully divine and fully human. (John 1:14; Phil. 2:5–8; Heb. 1:3; Heb. 4:15; Rom. 9:5)

sections.heading: Selected Canons and Significance
sections.html:
Selected Canons and Significance

The council issued thirty canons on church order, of which several remain historically significant:

Canon 9. If any clergyman has a grievance against another clergyman, he shall not forsake his own bishop and betake himself to secular courts, but shall first try his case before his own bishop, or with the agreement of the bishop, a tribunal chosen by both parties. Whoever acts contrary to this let him be subject to penalties.

Canon 14. Since in some provinces lectors and cantors have been allowed to marry, the holy synod decrees that it shall not be lawful for any of them to take a wife who is of a strange faith. But those who have already begotten children of such a marriage, if they have already had their children baptized among heretics, shall present them to the communion of the Catholic Church; but if they have not been baptized, they may not hereafter baptize them among heretics, nor give them in marriage to a heretic.

Canon 28. We also decree the same things as to the prerogatives of the most holy Church of Constantinople, which is New Rome. For the Fathers rightly granted privileges to the throne of old Rome, because it was the royal city. And the one hundred and fifty most religious bishops [at Constantinople, 381], actuated by the same consideration, gave equal privileges to the most holy throne of New Rome, judging that the city which is honoured with the Sovereignty and the Senate, and enjoys equal privileges with the old imperial Rome, should in ecclesiastical matters also be magnified as she is, and rank next after her.

Note: Canon 28's elevation of Constantinople was protested by Pope Leo I, foreshadowing the eventual East-West ecclesiastical division.

The Chalcedonian Definition remains the touchstone of orthodox Christology. It was rejected by the Oriental Orthodox churches (Coptic, Ethiopian, Armenian, Syrian) who hold a miaphysite Christology, and these churches remain separated from both Rome and Constantinople to this day. For the majority of world Christianity, however, the formula of Chalcedon — one person, two natures — is the irreducible grammar of who Jesus Christ is. (1 John 4:2; 2 John 7)
